text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Anita Bhogal
Riverside Health Centre

22 Millstream Way, Southwark, London, SE1 3DN
Dear Dr Anita Bhogal
Michael Carter, DOB: 14-05-1987, Hospital No: H7293184 NHS No. 6574930021
Flat 3, 14 Bramble Close, London, SE5 2HT 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. As you know, he has a history of episodic headaches consistent with migraine; however, the current focus of care remains his seizure disorder. I last assessed him on 10 June 2025, at which point we agreed on close monitoring and optimisation of adherence without introducing further medication changes.

Since that visit, he reports brief focal behavioural arrests with impaired awareness most evenings, typically clustering in the late evening before sleep; he estimates three to four events over the course of a week, predominantly towards bedtime rather than during the day. He also describes two longer convulsive events in the past three months, both occurring after sleep deprivation and missed meals. He denies tongue biting or incontinence with the shorter episodes, but his partner has noted brief post-event confusion. No clear daytime triggers aside from stress were identified. He has not been driving.

Current treatment comprises Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily and Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily, both well tolerated aside from intermittent irritability which he associates with work-related stress. He uses Sumatriptan 50 mg as required for migraine once or twice per month with good effect; there has been no temporal association between triptan use and the evening seizure clusters. He reports full adherence over the last eight weeks, supported by a pill organiser and mobile reminders.

Neurological examination today was unremarkable. Routine bloods from August 2025 (FBC, U&E, LFT, TFT, Vitamin D) were within reference ranges. MRI brain (2023) was non-lesional. A previous routine EEG (2024) showed non-specific changes without clear epileptiform discharges. Given the predominance of evening events and ongoing uncertainty regarding the underlying substrate, I have requested ambulatory video-EEG with overnight recording to better characterise the habitual spells. I have also provided a seizure diary template to capture timing (particularly the late-evening pattern), potential precipitants, and any associated automatisms.

We discussed safety and lifestyle measures, including sleep regularity, limiting alcohol, and ensuring medication is taken consistently with evening meals. He will remain off driving in accordance with DVLA guidance and understands the need to notify the DVLA if seizures continue. I have not altered medication today pending the ambulatory study, but if the record demonstrates clear epileptiform activity with persistent evening clustering, we will consider cautious up-titration of Lamotrigine or the introduction of a bedtime add-on therapy. He is agreeable to this plan.

I will review him after the ambulatory video-EEG, or sooner if he experiences a significant change in pattern, notably daytime convulsions or injury. Please feel free to contact me if there are interim concerns.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: he estimates three to four events over the course of a week

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes current seizure activity: brief focal events occurring in clusters most evenings, with an explicit estimate of "three to four events over the course of a week." Additionally, there were two longer convulsive seizures in the past three months (~0.67 per month). Per the rule to choose the highest frequency, 3 to 4 per week is higher than 2 per 3 months. Although clusters are mentioned, there is no data on clusters per period or events per cluster, so the standard frequency format is used.
seizure_frequency_number: he estimates three to four events over the course of a week

flaws.description: Gold (3 to 4 per week) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
